mappings.rationale: Stow's VSI directly covers the oceans including their physical and chemical properties, ocean currents, tides, waves, and the distribution of saltwater bodies.
Finney covers the physical and chemical properties of water, the hydrologic cycle, and water's role in the Earth system—central topics of this section.

mappings.vsiAuthor: David George Bowers, Emyr Martyn Roberts
mappings.rationale: Directly covers tides, tidal currents, and the forces causing them, corresponding to the section's detailed treatment of tides and tidal currents in the hydrosphere.
Vincent covers freshwater lake systems including their physical and chemical properties, directly relevant to the section's treatment of freshwater bodies.
Middleton covers river systems as a major component of the freshwater hydrosphere, relevant to the section's discussion of runoff and the hydrologic cycle.
Evans covers ice sheets, glaciers, and icebergs, directly relevant to the section's treatment of ice masses and their role in the hydrologic cycle.
Woodward covers ice sheets, glacial processes, and the role of ice in the hydrological system, relevant to the section's discussion of ice masses and pack ice.
Covers Arctic ice masses, pack ice, and the role of polar oceans in global ocean circulation, relevant to the section's treatment of ice and ocean currents.
Dodds covers Antarctic ice sheets, icebergs, and the Southern Ocean's role in global ocean circulation and the hydrologic cycle.
Maslin discusses ocean-atmosphere interactions, El Nino/Southern Oscillation, sea-level changes, and the ocean's role as a carbon dioxide sink—all topics in this section.
Covers the ocean-atmosphere system, including radiation and heat budgets, ENSO, and the Gulf Stream's moderating effects on climate discussed in this section.
Redfern provides context for the hydrosphere within the Earth system, including ocean circulation and the water cycle.
Lenton covers the hydrosphere as a key component of the Earth system, including ocean-atmosphere interactions and the global water cycle.
Goldsmith covers wave physics relevant to surface waves, ocean waves, tsunamis, and internal waves in the hydrosphere.
Sheppard covers marine environments and the physical and chemical properties of seawater in coral reef ecosystems, relevant to the section's treatment of ocean properties.
Mladenov discusses the marine environment's physical and chemical conditions, relevant to the section's coverage of seawater properties and ocean-atmosphere interactions.
McGuire covers tsunamis, storm surges, and sea-level rise, relevant to the section's discussion of waves and the effects of human activities on the hydrosphere.
Smith addresses the economic impacts of water pollution, acidification, and climate-induced disruption of the water balance discussed in this section.
Ali discusses sustainable water resource management, relevant to the section's coverage of water resources and the effects of human activities on the hydrosphere.
